title: Input Data Risks and Mitigations
concept:
Input data quality directly determines model quality — poor data consistently produces poor models regardless of algorithmic sophistication. Understanding the mapping between specific data risks and their testing mitigations is foundational to AI testing.

| **Risk** | **Testing Mitigation** |
|---|---|
| Bias in training data | Bias testing (disparate impact analysis, counterfactual testing) |
| Untrustworthy data sources | Data provenance testing (verify source legitimacy and integrity) |
| Poisoned data | A/B testing to detect anomalous behaviour changes; red teaming |
| Inconsistent/out-of-range data | Dataset constraint testing (range, type, null checks) |
| Sub-optimal features | Feature testing (correlation analysis, feature importance evaluation) |
| Imbalanced dataset | Representativeness testing (class distribution analysis, stratified sampling) |
| Poor labelling | Label correctness testing (expert review, inter-annotator agreement) |
| Data pipeline failures | Data pipeline testing (component + integration + system testing of the pipeline) |

Each risk type requires different testing techniques and occurs at different points in the ML workflow. Data provenance testing verifies the chain of custody from raw collection to training; pipeline testing verifies the transformation logic.

A data quality risk log should be created during EDA and maintained throughout development. Identified risks determine which automated data quality gates should be embedded in the CI/CD pipeline.
keyPoints: Each input data risk maps to a specific testing mitigation: biased data → bias testing; out-of-range data → dataset constraint testing; poor labels → label correctness testing.
Data poisoning (adversarial manipulation of training data) is mitigated by A/B testing to detect anomalous model behaviour changes and red teaming the training pipeline.
Data pipeline failures require testing at component, integration, and system levels — the pipeline is software and must be tested as such.

quiz.question: A team discovers that their sentiment analysis model performs significantly worse on reviews written by non-native English speakers compared to native speakers. Which input data risk does this most likely indicate?
options: Data pipeline failures — the pipeline is incorrectly transforming non-native text.
Bias in training data — the training dataset likely contained disproportionately fewer non-native speaker examples.
Poisoned data — an attacker has inserted adversarial examples targeting non-native speaker text.
Out-of-range data — non-native speaker text contains characters outside the expected range.
quiz.explanation: Significantly worse performance on a demographic subgroup (non-native speakers) is the classic signature of training data bias — the model has learned from data that under-represents that group. The mitigation is bias testing with counterfactual inputs and representativeness analysis of the training data composition.
